spans.content: ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5
Scoring the Short-Answer Case History Questions

Short-answer responses are scored on a range from 0 to 2. First, this section presents the criteria for scoring short-answer responses to the PLT case histories. Then, it shows how three different levels of short answers to the same question might be scored as a 2, a 1, and a 0 based on the scoring criteria that follow.
Criteria for Scoring
For a score of 2, the response must
■ show the writer has a thorough understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address all components of the question.
■ provide a strong explanation (if requested) and support it with relevant evidence.
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses strong knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
CHAPTER
ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5
Scoring the Short-Answer Case History Questions

Short-answer responses are scored on a range from 0 to 2. First, this section presents the criteria for scoring short-answer responses to the PLT case histories. Then, it shows how three different levels of short answers to the same question might be scored as a 2, a 1, and a 0 based on the scoring criteria that follow.
Criteria for Scoring
For a score of 2, the response must
■ show the writer has a thorough understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address all components of the question.
■ provide a strong explanation (if requested) and support it with relevant evidence.
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses strong knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
For a score of 1, the response must
■ show the writer has a basic understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address one component of the question.
■ provide a weak explanation (if requested) which is still supported with relevant evidence
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
For a score of 0, the response must
■ show that the writer misunderstood the parts of the case history that relate to the question
■ fail to address the question
■ not be supported by relevant evidence
■ provide little evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question
Answers and Explanations
PLT Case History 5.1 Ms. Morris

1. First, Ms. Morris should not be judgmental of the parent or Alicja based on the note's contents and composition flaws. She should reflect on the purpose of the note, the context of the situation, and what this all means in regard to Alicja's education and her role in it. As a first-year teacher, Ms. Morris may want to bring the note to her administrator and/or school social worker before responding to the parent or talking with Alicja. Administrators and social workers usually understand the needs of the community in which the school resides and can

shed some light on the situation. Also, if Ms. Morris is unfamiliar with the community environment, family circumstances, and economic conditions that might warrant Alicja babysitting while her parent works, the administrator or social worker can help her think about how to respond. Second, this note might be a cause for Ms. Morris to reflect on just how much and the purpose for the homework she is assigning each night. Homework should not be busy work. It should be purposeful. It might be best to change the homework routine or to talk with Alicja about other times during the school day she has available to work on homework. Perhaps she is not using a study hall time advantageously to complete her homework before she even gets home. Respectful reflection, discussion, and planning should help solve this dilemma.

2. Over the school year, Ms. Morris could develop active partnerships by encouraging respectful, reciprocal communication among her students, their families, and herself. Many teachers only contact families when there is something negative to report about their child's progress or school work. Ms. Morris and her students could create opportunities to invite the families into the classroom for positive interactions. Together they could come up with a number of ideas that would welcome families to the classroom. For example, students could compose poetry and invite families in for a poetry slam. A class newsletter written by the students and Ms. Morris could keep parents informed about what is going on in the classroom. On the other hand, Ms. Morris needs to invite families to communicate their needs and concerns to her. At every opportunity she can, she should ask family members to keep her informed. Another way Ms. Morris can cultivate active partnerships is to practice shared decision-making with her students and their families. When possible and appropriate, Ms. Morris should ask others'

For a score of 1, the response must
■ show the writer has a basic understanding of the parts of the case history that relate to the question.
■ appropriately address one component of the question.
■ provide a weak explanation (if requested) which is still supported with relevant evidence
■ provide evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question.
For a score of 0, the response must
■ show that the writer misunderstood the parts of the case history that relate to the question
■ fail to address the question
■ not be supported by relevant evidence
■ provide little evidence that the writer possesses some knowledge of pedagogical concepts, facts, theories, methodologies, or procedures relevant to the question
Answers and Explanations
PLT Case History 5.1 Ms. Morris

1. First, Ms. Morris should not be judgmental of the parent or Alicja based on the note's contents and composition flaws. She should reflect on the purpose of the note, the context of the situation, and what this all means in regard to Alicja's education and her role in it. As a first-year teacher, Ms. Morris may want to bring the note to her administrator and/or school social worker before responding to the parent or talking with Alicja. Administrators and social workers usually understand the needs of the community in which the school resides and can

shed some light on the situation. Also, if Ms. Morris is unfamiliar with the community environment, family circumstances, and economic conditions that might warrant Alicja babysitting while her parent works, the administrator or social worker can help her think about how to respond. Second, this note might be a cause for Ms. Morris to reflect on just how much and the purpose for the homework she is assigning each night. Homework should not be busy work. It should be purposeful. It might be best to change the homework routine or to talk with Alicja about other times during the school day she has available to work on homework. Perhaps she is not using a study hall time advantageously to complete her homework before she even gets home. Respectful reflection, discussion, and planning should help solve this dilemma.

2. Over the school year, Ms. Morris could develop active partnerships by encouraging respectful, reciprocal communication among her students, their families, and herself. Many teachers only contact families when there is something negative to report about their child's progress or school work. Ms. Morris and her students could create opportunities to invite the families into the classroom for positive interactions. Together they could come up with a number of ideas that would welcome families to the classroom. For example, students could compose poetry and invite families in for a poetry slam. A class newsletter written by the students and Ms. Morris could keep parents informed about what is going on in the classroom. On the other hand, Ms. Morris needs to invite families to communicate their needs and concerns to her. At every opportunity she can, she should ask family members to keep her informed. Another way Ms. Morris can cultivate active partnerships is to practice shared decision-making with her students and their families. When possible and appropriate, Ms. Morris should ask others'
opinions and elicit their ideas and suggestions. Incorporating their concerns into the curriculum and daily classroom practices will confirm they are important.
opinions and elicit their ideas and suggestions. Incorporating their concerns into the curriculum and daily classroom practices will confirm they are important.

3. The National Education Association's Code of Ethics consists of two principles. Principle I is the Commitment to the Student. The principle states that educators should do their best to help students realize their potential as a member of society. To do this, the educator should stimulate the student to access multiple and various points of view, treat all students fairly, and attempt to protect the student from harmful conditions to learning, health, and safety. Principle II is Commitment to the Profession. The principle states the educator should use good judgment and should not knowingly make false statements about a colleague or accept any gifts that might influence his professional actions.
PLT Case History 5.2 Mr. Tenace

1. Mr. Tenace's project plan depicts these two strengths of effective planning. His plan, which is based on a state science learning standard, is well sequenced and provides various grouping opportunities. Students will construct understandings of the living systems by reading widely about them from self-selected texts and sources. They will bring what they learn to a class discussion. They will work with the support of others in small groups to illustrate one system; then, they will illustrate the other system independently. This provides the opportunity for students to learn in the safety of a small group and then practice the process independently. The plan provides modeling and supportive grouping as well as opportunities to work independently, reflect, and self-assess. These are all recommended aspects of effective planning.
2. Mr. Tenace could try to equalize the roles of males and females in his science classes by mod-

eling equal treatment of both sexes and by assigning same-sex lab partners. Based on Mrs. Parker's observation, it appears Mr. Tenace perhaps unintentionally models unequal treatment of the sexes. He refers to the females as "young ladies" that we don't want to hurt and doesn't address the boys' banter about there being no ladies present. In essence, he is modeling for the boys that the girls are different and should be treated differently in his class. When asked if the girls had to participate in the dissection, he sidesteps the issue by saying partners can make these decisions. Mr. Tenace should set high expectations of participation for all of his students and discuss the value of being knowledgeable about science. He could also try assigning same-sex lab partners. He stumbled upon this possible anecdote during his interviews with the students. It appears some girls relinquish some of their power to the boys during labs and take on a helpless role themselves. It might be helpful to observe the dynamics that occur between same-sex partners.

3. Mr. Tenace should search for articles about gender issues in professional journals. He could consult not only education journals but psychology journals. Reading up on the research on gender, particularly issues related to gender and the field of science, will help Mr. Tenace reflect on what is going on in his classroom. Applying the readings to his classes can guide him to assess his practices with and expectations for all students. Also, Mr. Tenace should start a reflective teaching journal in which he writes about his feelings about gender issues and the sciences. He could explore how his words and actions provide models for others in the class and how he could rectify any misguided actions he has been practicing. Finally, he could consult with colleagues, such as Mrs. Parker. Colleagues can share their perspectives and offer guidance and suggestions.
ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5

3. The National Education Association's Code of Ethics consists of two principles. Principle I is the Commitment to the Student. The principle states that educators should do their best to help students realize their potential as a member of society. To do this, the educator should stimulate the student to access multiple and various points of view, treat all students fairly, and attempt to protect the student from harmful conditions to learning, health, and safety. Principle II is Commitment to the Profession. The principle states the educator should use good judgment and should not knowingly make false statements about a colleague or accept any gifts that might influence his professional actions.
PLT Case History 5.2 Mr. Tenace

1. Mr. Tenace's project plan depicts these two strengths of effective planning. The plan provides modeling and supportive grouping as well as opportunities to work independently, reflect, and self-assess. These are all recommended aspects of effective planning.

eling equal treatment of both sexes and by assigning same-sex lab partners. Based on Mrs. Parker's observation, it appears Mr. Tenace perhaps unintentionally models unequal treatment of the sexes. He stumbled upon this possible anecdote during his interviews with the students. It appears some girls relinquish some of their power to the boys during labs and take on a helpless role themselves. It might be helpful to observe the dynamics that occur between same-sex partners.

3. Mr. Tenace should search for articles about gender issues in professional journals. He could consult not only education journals but psychology journals. Reading up on the research on gender, particularly issues related to gender and the field of science, will help Mr. Tenace reflect on what is going on in his classroom. Applying the readings to his classes can guide him to assess his practices with and expectations for all students. Also, Mr. Tenace should start a reflective teaching journal in which he writes about his feelings about gender issues and the sciences. He could explore how his words and actions provide models for others in the class and how he could rectify any misguided actions he has been practicing. Finally, he could consult with colleagues, such as Mrs. Parker. Colleagues can share their perspectives and offer guidance and suggestions.
PLT Case History 5.3 Kim

1. Based on what Ms. Lee has observed so far, in what areas should she begin assessing Kim? How should Ms. Lee proceed in assessing Kim's needs? Ms. Lee should continue observing and assessing Kim, especially her ability to hear, listen to, and understand oral conversations and directions. Ms. Lee should check Kim's permanent school record to see if there has been any history of hearing loss in the past. She should check to see that a hearing examination was completed recently. If not, she may ask the school nurse if he or she could check into the matter. Ms. Lee should continue to observe Kim's behaviors to determine if Kim responds appropriately to one-on-one conversations. It may be Kim has difficulty focusing in large-group situations. Or perhaps she would benefit from having her seat moved to a quieter or more central place in the room. Ms. Lee should talk with Kim to inquire if there is anything she can do to help Kim. Ms. Lee should arrange for a parent-teacher conference in which she would share her concerns in a sensitive way, not to alarm the parent but to gain insight. She may even consult Kim's past teachers.

2. After further assessment, Ms. Lee has determined that Kim would benefit from learning/listening strategies to help her be a more active listener. What are two listening strategies Ms. Lee could teach Kim? Two listening strategies that might help Kim develop her active listening skills are:

a. Avoid physical distractions. Kim should learn to cut out physical distractions so she can focus on the content of the message. She may find closing her eyes and visualizing a story as it is being read will help her focus. Or she should try to look directly at the speaker's eyes or mouth and think about what the speaker is saying. If she does lose

track of what the speaker is saying, she should just pick up the conversation or story from where the speaker is right then. It is better to re-focus on the rest of the story than just give up. She will probably still be able to pick up what is being said even after a lapse in active listening.
b. Write down key words the speaker is saying to keep track of the steps, sequence, or procedure. It may be helpful to graphically organize thoughts in a personally meaningful way as she listens.

3. Ms. Lee's school follows a typical Response to Intervention model. What is Response to Intervention? Explain the three tiers of the model. Response to Intervention (RTI) is an early-detection support system that helps teachers identify and help students who are struggling. Its framework helps teachers make instructional decisions based on data collected on all students. First, all students are screened using a universal assessment tool; then students are placed into three tiers of instruction based on their needs. Tier 1 represents students who are roughly at or above grade level. They are least likely to need intervention. Tier 2 represents students who are behind their peers and need some form of intervention. Tier 3 represents students who are one or more years behind their peers and require extensive intervention. All students receive the interventional instruction appropriate to their tier, and their progress is continually monitored to ensure they are doing well.

Mr. Vanderwerker

She may find closing her eyes and visualizing a story as it is being read will help her focus. Or she should try to look directly at the speaker's eyes or mouth and think about what the speaker is saying. If she does lose

3. Ms. Lee's school follows a typical Response to Intervention model. What is Response to Intervention? Explain the three tiers of the model. Response to Intervention (RTI) is an early-detection support system that helps teachers identify and help students who are struggling. Its framework helps teachers make instructional decisions based on data collected on all students. First, all students are screened using a universal assessment tool; then students are placed into three tiers of instruction based on their needs. Tier 1 represents students who are roughly at or above grade level. They are least likely to need intervention. Tier 2 represents students who are behind their peers and need some form of intervention. Tier 3 represents students who are one or more years behind their peers and require extensive intervention. All students receive the interventional instruction appropriate to their tier, and their progress is continually monitored to ensure they are doing well.
PLT Case History 5.4
Mr. Vanderwerker
1. Mr. Vanderwerker might use small-group instruction to differentiate his instruction to meet the range of reading needs of individuals by assigning the class to small working groups to

complete particular tasks. For example, he might assign small groups so that each cluster includes students at a variety of reading levels. Students would use resources appropriate to their reading level to contribute information to a group report. Students would construct knowledge using their strengths and preferred learning styles to contribute to the group. He might also provide instruction on negotiating texts and specific reading strategies in homogeneous groups of students who are having difficulty with a particular skill such as using a text's index or the headings and subheadings in a text to find information.

complete particular tasks. For example, he might assign small groups so that each cluster includes students at a variety of reading levels. Students would use resources appropriate to their reading level to contribute information to a group report. Students would construct knowledge using their strengths and preferred learning styles to contribute to the group. He might also provide instruction on negotiating texts and specific reading strategies in homogeneous groups of students who are having difficulty with a particular skill such as using a text's index or the headings and subheadings in a text to find information.

2. The zone of proximal development refers to the distance between the student's ability to independently solve a problem and the student's potential development determined by the student's ability to solve a problem with assistance from a more knowledgeable peer or adult. Students work within the ZPD when assistance is needed to complete a task. The assistance teaches the student to internalize the skill or strategy. Vygotsky theorized that real teaching and learning occurs within the students' ZPD. Vygotsky's theory of ZPD suggests teachers work with students in small groups by clarifying that small groups should be formed to instruct students in a skill or strategy that they cannot solve by themselves yet and need the guidance of a more knowledgeable peer or adult. The adult or peer would help the student internalize the skill or strategy to the point where he or she could eventually perform or understand it independently. Vygotsky would probably say that small groups should be formed so students are working within their ZPD. That may mean either that groups are homogeneously grouped so everyone is working at the same ZPD at the same level or that they are heterogeneously grouped and more knowledgeable peers are assisting peers who are working at their ZPD.

3. Well in advance of introducing the themed unit in the classroom, Mr. Vanderwerker should approach the school librarian and ask for assistance in finding appropriate materials for the unit. Through interlibrary loan and Internet research, the librarian and teacher should be able to find a wealth of resources at various reading levels. Mr. Vanderwerker should keep in mind also that students learn not only from reading material but from photographs, videos, and virtual field trips. Many types of resources are available and downloadable through the Internet. Finally, if the school has funding to purchase new materials for the classroom or library each year, that money could be earmarked for this unit. Otherwise, the teacher and librarian could seek out external institutions that offer grants and funding for classroom materials. The teacher may also be willing to purchase books for his students using his own money.
Multiple-Choice Questions I
1. c. A norm-referenced test does all of the things listed in the description of what Mr. Fortuin is describing to the parent.
2. d. Piaget's concrete operational stage is characterized by logical and concrete reasoning, organized thinking, the loss of egocentric thinking, and an inability to handle abstract reasoning.
3. a. Gagne's "The Conditions of Learning" outlined the relation of appropriate instructional designs to learning objectives. There is no evidence that any of the other choices are accurate.
ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5

Multiple-Choice Questions I
1. c. A norm-referenced test does all of the things listed in the description of what Mr. Fortuin is describing to the parent.
2. d. Piaget's concrete operational stage is characterized by logical and concrete reasoning, organized thinking, the loss of egocentric thinking, and an inability to handle abstract reasoning.
3. a. Gagne's "The Conditions of Learning" outlined the relation of appropriate instructional designs to learning objectives. There is no evidence that any of the other choices are accurate.

4. d. The No Child Left Behind Act of 2001 supports standards-based education reform. Goals 2000, which identified eight national educational goals for U.S. students that were to be achieved by the year 2000, stated all children in the United States would start school ready to learn.

5. c. In writing workshop approaches, students select topics and participate in the writing process by reading their drafts to peers, conferring with others, and learning what it is like to be a writer and author.
6. a. Constructivism views learning as a process whereby learners actively construct new concepts.

7. b. Accommodations are adjustments to presentation, responses, setting, timing, and pacing that help children with special needs participate in regular classrooms. They do not, however, lower the level of difficulty or the standards of achievement. Not all children with special needs require accommodations.
8. c. Scientifically based research is required by schools seeking to use federal funds to support educational programs according to the No Child Left Behind Act of 2001.
9. d. The practices described are all excellent examples of differentiated instruction that will help the teacher respond to variances among skill levels in his classroom.
10. a. Discovery learning, a constructivist approach to learning, is based on inquiry. Jerome Bruner and John Dewey's philosophies of learning support discovery learning.

11. a. Divergent thinking involves the ability to think creatively and brainstorm many answers or solutions. The best example is choice a because all of the other questions have “right” and “wrong” answers.

12. b. While the six kingdoms are accurate, the concept map does not show the student understood the map meant to show the connections between the concepts he filled in. Concept maps illustrate the relationships among ideas. "The Six Kingdoms of Biology" heading should be in the center with each kingdom in a circle around the main heading. There is no evidence the student copied the information.
Multiple-Choice Questions II

1. c. Culturally responsive teaching is characterized by instruction that encourages children to understand and welcome their own and other's cultures. Multiculturalism should be integrated into all subjects and skills rather than focused on during a special themed unit. Children should be viewed as individuals, not representatives of their race expected to respond to questions about the race in general.

2. a. Direct instruction emphasizes carefully planned lessons that are designed around very small increments of learning. Often direct instruction involves the use of a script. It is based on the philosophy that when taught properly, all children can learn.

3. d. Choice d encourages the student to reflect on how his actions affected his mother. His response to this question will most likely help him understand he is the one in control of his mother's reaction. If he wants to make his mother proud next time, he needs to take different action.
ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5

4. d. The No Child Left Behind Act of 2001 supports standards-based education reform. Goals 2000, which identified eight national educational goals for U.S. students that were to be achieved by the year 2000, stated all children in the United States would start school ready to learn.

5. c. In writing workshop approaches, students select topics and participate in the writing process by reading their drafts to peers, conferring with others, and learning what it is like to be a writer and author.
6. a. Constructivism views learning as a process whereby learners actively construct new concepts.

7. b. Accommodations are adjustments to presentation, responses, setting, timing, and pacing that help children with special needs participate in regular classrooms. They do not, however, lower the level of difficulty or the standards of achievement. Not all children with special needs require accommodations.
8. c. Scientifically based research is required by schools seeking to use federal funds to support educational programs according to the No Child Left Behind Act of 2001.
9. d. The practices described are all excellent examples of differentiated instruction that will help the teacher respond to variances among skill levels in his classroom.
10. a. Discovery learning, a constructivist approach to learning, is based on inquiry. Jerome Bruner and John Dewey's philosophies of learning support discovery learning.

11. a. Divergent thinking involves the ability to think creatively and brainstorm many answers or solutions. The best example is choice a because all of the other questions have “right” and “wrong” answers.

Concept maps illustrate the relationships among ideas. "The Six Kingdoms of Biology" heading should be in the center with each kingdom in a circle around the main heading. There is no evidence the student copied the information.
Multiple-Choice Questions II

1. c. Culturally responsive teaching is characterized by instruction that encourages children to understand and welcome their own and other's cultures. Multiculturalism should be integrated into all subjects and skills rather than focused on during a special themed unit. Children should be viewed as individuals, not representatives of their race expected to respond to questions about the race in general.

2. a. Direct instruction emphasizes carefully planned lessons that are designed around very small increments of learning. Often direct instruction involves the use of a script. It is based on the philosophy that when taught properly, all children can learn.

3. d. Choice d encourages the student to reflect on how his actions affected his mother. His response to this question will most likely help him understand he is the one in control of his mother's reaction. If he wants to make his mother proud next time, he needs to take different action.

4. a. The complexity of a concept might require more time to ensure all students understand the lesson. Unfortunately, teachers and students are not limited to teaching and learning only those things they hold particular interest in. Grade 5–9 curriculum can mandate lessons that are required content.
5. d. Choice d encourages the male to think about the female's reaction to the term rather than demanding he apologize or punishing him with a writing assignment.
6. a. Children are motivated by choice; therefore, choice a provides the most options for student selection. Choice a allows students to take ownership and engage themselves in meaningful learning.
7. c. Self-actualization refers to one's desire to be self-fulfilled.
8. b. Readers gain a great deal of information about a text by previewing it and making predictions about its contents before reading.
9. b. Deductive thinking moves from specific observations to broader theories and conclusions.

10. d. When in doubt, a teacher should always consult the building administrator who may be aware of policies and circumstances in which information should or should not be shared. In general, it is best not to speak of school matters outside of school and to guard the confidentiality of one's students and their families.
11. c. The author of the first passage would most likely agree national standards should be set and adhered to by all states so that consistency exists across the nation.
12. d. Choice d is the only response that is feasible. The second passage suggests state control takes into consideration the economic needs of a state when it refers to funding.
Praxis II Scoring

Evaluate how you did on this practice exam by scoring your short-answer responses and your multiple-choice answers. Bring your short-answer responses to a trusted friend to score so you can better see how you did. Reflect on the types of questions you missed most frequently.

The key to doing well on the Praxis II is to practice, read widely, and stay organized and focused. You have self-assessed your strengths and weaknesses and learned how to manage the various types of questions on the test. Enter the exam with confidence, knowing you are ready and equipped to do your very best!
ANSWERS AND EXPLANATIONS FOR PRACTICE EXAM 5

4. a. The complexity of a concept might require more time to ensure all students understand the lesson. Unfortunately, teachers and students are not limited to teaching and learning only those things they hold particular interest in. Grade 5–9 curriculum can mandate lessons that are required content.
5. d. Choice d encourages the male to think about the female's reaction to the term rather than demanding he apologize or punishing him with a writing assignment.
6. a. Children are motivated by choice; therefore, choice a provides the most options for student selection. Choice a allows students to take ownership and engage themselves in meaningful learning.
7. c. Self-actualization refers to one's desire to be self-fulfilled.
8. b. Readers gain a great deal of information about a text by previewing it and making predictions about its contents before reading.
9. b. Deductive thinking moves from specific observations to broader theories and conclusions.

10. d. When in doubt, a teacher should always consult the building administrator who may be aware of policies and circumstances in which information should or should not be shared. In general, it is best not to speak of school matters outside of school and to guard the confidentiality of one's students and their families.
11. c. The author of the first passage would most likely agree national standards should be set and adhered to by all states so that consistency exists across the nation.
12. d. Choice d is the only response that is feasible. The second passage suggests state control takes into consideration the economic needs of a state when it refers to funding.
Praxis II Scoring

Evaluate how you did on this practice exam by scoring your short-answer responses and your multiple-choice answers. Bring your short-answer responses to a trusted friend to score so you can better see how you did. Reflect on the types of questions you missed most frequently.

The key to doing well on the Praxis II is to practice, read widely, and stay organized and focused. You have self-assessed your strengths and weaknesses and learned how to manage the various types of questions on the test. Enter the exam with confidence, knowing you are ready and equipped to do your very best!
